articles.title: Except for These Chains
articles.dek: A man in chains prayed that the king judging him would have what he had. The difference between them had nothing to do with power.
articles.quote: The chains Paul wore were iron and temporary. The chains of 'almost' can last a lifetime.
articles.body:
You know someone like this. Maybe you have been praying for them for years. They show up to Bible study. They sing the worship songs. They nod at the right moments, and sometimes they will even say, There might be something to this Jesus thing. But when the moment comes — when you finally ask, So, are you ready? — there is always one more question, one more week, one more reason to wait. Malapit na. Konti na lang. Almost. But not quite.

The man sitting across from Paul in Acts 26 was exactly like this.

It is the year 59 A.D. Paul is standing in a grand hall in Caesarea Maritima — a Roman city built to impress, with a great harbor, marble columns, and the kind of architecture designed to remind visitors who was in charge. Before him sits Herod Agrippa II, the last of the Herodian kings, wearing royal purple, flanked by Festus the governor and a procession of military commanders. The room glitters with power. Paul is in chains.

He has been in custody for two years. He has survived riots, assassination plots, a shipwreck, and a snake bite. He has already appealed to Caesar, which means this hearing does not technically matter anymore — he is going to Rome regardless. And yet Agrippa wanted to hear him speak. So Paul speaks.

He tells the story of his life: the trained Pharisee who enforced Jewish law with violent zeal, who stood by as Stephen was stoned, who hunted down followers of Jesus from city to city. He says it plainly — “I too was convinced that I ought to do all that was possible to oppose the name of Jesus of Nazareth” (Acts 26:9). He was not a halfhearted enemy. He was thorough.

Then he describes the Damascus road. He does it with the vividness of a man who replays it constantly — the light brighter than the sun, the fall to the ground, the voice: “Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me? It is hard for you to kick against the goads” (Acts 26:14). A goad is the sharp stick used to drive oxen. The image is precise. Paul had been fighting something he could not stop, a force that was only wounding him in the resistance. That voice changed everything. He was commissioned to carry the good news — “to open their eyes and turn them from darkness to light” (Acts 26:18). He obeyed. He preached. And that, he says, is exactly why the Jewish leaders seized him in the temple and why he stands in chains today.

He is mid-speech when Festus interrupts — loudly. “You are out of your mind, Paul! Your great learning is driving you insane!” (Acts 26:24). It is a fascinating moment. Festus does not dispute Paul's facts. He does not call him a liar. He just calls him crazy. This is what power often does with a truth it cannot answer — it does not argue, it dismisses.

Paul does not flinch. “I am not insane, most excellent Festus. What I am saying is true and reasonable” (Acts 26:25). And then he turns — and this is the dramatic turn — he turns directly to the king: “King Agrippa, do you believe the prophets? I know you do” (Acts 26:27). It is a bold, almost reckless move. Paul is essentially saying: You have read the scrolls. You know the prophecies. You already know this is true.

Agrippa does not deny it. But he will not fully commit either. And so he says something that has echoed through two thousand years of biblical commentary: “Do you think that in such a short time you can persuade me to be a Christian?” (Acts 26:28).

Almost. But not quite.

“Short time or long — I pray to God that not only you but all who are listening to me today may become what I am, except for these chains.” — Acts 26:29, NIV

Notice what Paul does not do. He does not argue harder. He does not produce more evidence. He does not get flustered or give up. He prays — out loud, in front of the king, in front of the governor, in front of the whole room. He says: I want you to have what I have.

Think about that. Paul is standing before a man who lives in a palace, wears purple, and commands armies. Paul has been in a cell for two years. He has scars. He is in chains. And he looks at the king and says: I want you to be like me.

What does Paul have that Agrippa does not?

Not comfort. Not safety. Not freedom of movement. Paul has direction — a voice he heard on a road that reordered his entire life. He has a mission so clear that two years of imprisonment have not shaken it. He has the inexplicable peace of someone who knows exactly what he was made for and has stopped fighting it.

Agrippa has a throne. But Agrippa is almost persuaded. And almost, if you look at it honestly, is its own kind of chain. The man who is almost convinced cannot go back to simple ignorance — he has heard too much. He cannot move forward into full trust — something is holding him. He lives in the uncomfortable in-between, neither free nor committed. The chains Paul wore were iron and temporary. The chains of “almost” can last a lifetime.

Maybe you know this from the inside — not as Agrippa, but as the person praying for an Agrippa in your life. The ninong who always listens but never decides. The friend who says Oo naman, totoo naman yan and then goes home unchanged. The child who left the faith in college and keeps circling back to the edges without ever stepping all the way in.

Paul's prayer for Agrippa is permission to keep praying — boldly, specifically, without shame. He did not stop caring when Agrippa pushed back. He did not take the “almost” personally. He simply said, out loud, to God: Let him have what I have. Let the whole room have it. There is no bitterness in that prayer. There is only love.

And if you are your own Agrippa today — if you know more truth than you are currently living, if you have heard enough that you cannot un-hear it but still keep one foot at the door — hear this too: “almost” is not a safe place to stay. The person who half-trusts God carries the weight of negotiating everything alone, indefinitely, never fully landing anywhere.

The story ends with a quiet irony. After Paul speaks, Agrippa and Festus confer privately. Their conclusion is clear — “This man is not doing anything that deserves death or imprisonment” (Acts 26:31). And then Agrippa says to Festus: “This man could have been set free if he had not appealed to Caesar” (Acts 26:32). Agrippa had the power to free Paul. He was almost persuaded. He did nothing.

Paul went to Rome in chains — and changed the world anyway, because the chains on his wrists were never the real issue. God was not finished with him in a cell, and God would not have been finished with him had Agrippa set him free. The mission outlasted every verdict.

Whatever you are almost ready to step into today — almost ready to trust, almost ready to forgive, almost ready to say yes — take the step. Pray the prayer Paul prayed out loud in that glittering hall: Lord, let me have what people who fully trust You have. Not almost. All the way in.

articles.title: The Wrong Voice in the Room
articles.dek: Rehoboam inherited the greatest kingdom on earth and lost most of it in a single conversation — by listening to the wrong advisors.
articles.quote: Genuine authority is earned by those who serve, not seized by those who demand.
articles.body:
Picture this. You have just been promoted — maybe you are the new team lead, the new barangay captain, the newly installed department head — and barely a week into the role, a delegation arrives. It is the people you are now responsible for, and they are not here to celebrate. They are here with a burden they have been carrying for too long. Their request is not unreasonable: lighten the load a little, and we will give you everything we have. You stand at a fork in the road. To your left: the older voices, the people who have seen what happens to leaders who lose the people's trust. To your right: your barkada — the friends who cheered loudest when you got the position, now telling you to be strong, to show them who is in charge, to not let them take advantage of you. Which voice do you follow?

Which voice did Rehoboam follow?

The year is roughly 930 B.C. Solomon is dead. His son Rehoboam has inherited what was, at that moment, the most powerful kingdom in the ancient world — a realm of gold, cedar, trade routes, and a temple that made the Queen of Sheba catch her breath. All of it falls into Rehoboam's hands. And yet before the crown is even warm on his head, he faces the exact scene described above.

The whole assembly of Israel gathers at Shechem to make him king. They come with a spokesperson — Jeroboam, who had once fled from Solomon and only recently returned from Egypt. The people speak plainly: "Your father put a heavy yoke on us, but now lighten the harsh labor and the heavy yoke he put on us, and we will serve you." (2 Chronicles 10:4, NIV)

It was not a rebellion. It was a request. A reasonable one.

Rehoboam gives them three days and seeks counsel. He first turns to the elders — the men who had served alongside his father, who had watched how Solomon built and held a nation together for decades. Their advice is disarmingly simple:

"If you will be kind to these people and please them and give them a favorable answer, they will always be your servants." — 2 Chronicles 10:7 (NIV)

Serve them. And they will serve you forever.

But Rehoboam does not like that answer. He turns instead to the young men who had grown up with him — his peers, his inner circle, the friends who had never been tested by anything harder than a palace banquet. They give him the kind of advice that flatters a man but destroys a kingdom: tell the people that his little finger is thicker than his father's waist, that his father scourged them with whips but he will scourge them with scorpions. (2 Chronicles 10:10–11, NIV)

When Jeroboam and the assembly return three days later, Rehoboam delivers this speech. Word for brutal word.

The reaction is immediate and permanent. Ten of the twelve tribes tear themselves away that day. The united kingdom — built by Saul, expanded by David, made glorious by Solomon — shatters in a single conversation. Rehoboam keeps only Judah and Benjamin. He never gets the rest back.

Here is the thing worth sitting with: the elders were not being naive. They were not telling Rehoboam to be a pushover. They were giving him something strategically brilliant — the insight that the surest path to lasting authority runs through the people's trust, not their fear. Kindness was not the soft option. It was the wise one.

Rehoboam made the mistake that leaders across every era keep making: he confused harshness with strength. He thought that if he gave an inch, he would lose his grip. He could not see that he had already won — that an entire nation was standing in front of him, ready to swear loyalty, if only he would give them a reason to. He had everything. He threw it away for the satisfaction of looking powerful.

It is also worth noticing who he listened to. The elders had lived through the whole arc of Solomon's reign. They had seen what kept a kingdom together, and what quietly eroded it. The young men had only ambition and bravado — they had never governed anything, never been held accountable for anything. But they were close, familiar, fun. And they told Rehoboam what felt good to hear. That feeling cost him ten tribes.

We are all Rehoboam in some season. We are all handed something — a family, a business, a team, a classroom, a household — and tested early with a version of that same question: will you use this position to serve, or to be served?

In Filipino life, we know this test intimately. It shows up in the father who finally has financial breathing room and can either ease the family's burdens or use the moment to tighten his grip. It shows up in the small business owner who made it and somewhere along the way stopped being Ate to her staff. It shows up in the supervisor who was once warm in the cubicle and becomes unreachable once they move into the corner office. The yoke gets heavier. The scorpions replace the whips. And people quietly start looking for someone else to follow.

The elders' counsel cuts across centuries. Notice the word always in their advice. This is not a short-term play. It is the long game. Kindness given freely, authority worn lightly, loads lifted when you have the power to lift them — these things compound. They build something no amount of severity can manufacture: genuine loyalty. The kind that does not need threats to hold it together. Genuine authority is earned by those who serve, not seized by those who demand.

Think of someone under your care right now — your child, your employee, your sibling, your team member, your neighbor. Are they carrying something heavier than they need to? Is there a yoke on them that you have the power to ease — not everything, not all at once, but something? One word. One decision. One act of kindness that says: I see you. I know it is heavy. Let me help.

Rehoboam's story does not end in total ruin. God, in his mercy, stops him from going to war against the ten northern tribes (2 Chronicles 11:1–4), and he eventually builds something out of what remains. Grace finds him, even after the break. But he never gets those ten tribes back. Some losses, once made, are permanent. Some fractures never fully heal.

The good news is that you are reading this before your Shechem moment — or perhaps right in the middle of it. The elders' advice is still available to you. The wiser voice is still speaking. Choose it. Not because it is easier, but because it is what lasting authority is made of. Lift what you can lift. Give the people a favorable answer. And the loyalty you receive in return will be the kind that no scorpion could ever have purchased.

articles.title: The Prayer in the Ruins
articles.dek: Psalm 79 opens in ash — temple destroyed, dead unburied, neighbors mocking. But the psalmist does one quietly remarkable thing: he keeps talking to God.
articles.quote: You only cry out to someone you believe is listening. The lament is already the faith.
articles.body:
The monsoon has been falling since before dawn. Somewhere across Luzon today, classes are cancelled and the streets run brown with rainwater. It is a gray, low-ceilinged kind of morning — the kind that makes you want to stay in bed, draw the curtain, and wait for the sky to change its mind.

There is a grief that feels exactly like this. Not the sharp, shattering grief of sudden loss, but the heavy, steady grief of watching something sacred be destroyed — piece by piece, until nothing stands. The grief that does not rush. It just stays.

That is the grief of Psalm 79.

The year is 586 BC. Babylon's army has arrived at Jerusalem — and this was not simply a military defeat. This was a dismantling of everything that gave Israel her identity. The soldiers marched into the temple courts, the holiest ground Israel knew, and desecrated them. Smashed the sacred furniture. Melted down the gold. Set the whole complex ablaze. Then they turned on the city: the streets of Jerusalem were thick with the dead, and no one was left to bury them.

In the Philippines, we understand what this means at a gut level. The lamay, the all-night wake, the procession to the grave — these are not formality. They are how a community says: this person mattered. To leave the dead unburied is to strip them of that dignity entirely. In ancient Israel, as in Filipino culture, it was considered a curse. An ultimate dishonor.

That is the image the psalmist reaches for in verse 3, the most devastating line in this passage:

"They have poured out blood like water all around Jerusalem, and there is no one to bury the dead." (Psalm 79:3, NIV)

No one. Not a neighbor. Not a priest. Not a passerby who might stop long enough to show a little mercy.

Then verse 4 lands like a second blow: "We are objects of contempt to our neighbors, of scorn and derision to those around us." (Psalm 79:4, NIV). The surrounding nations — the ones who once envied Jerusalem's glory — were now pointing at the rubble. Some were laughing. The people who used to fear Israel were shaking their heads.

If you have ever felt that — the humiliation of collapse, witnessed by people who used to respect you — then this psalm was written for you.

Now here is the thing worth pausing on.

The psalmist is Asaph — one of the great worship leaders and musicians of the temple, the same temple now turned to ash. He has lost his vocation, his community, his holy ground. He has every reason to go silent. And yet — look at what he does.

He prays.

Notice his exact words in verse 1: "O God, the nations have invaded your inheritance; they have defiled your holy temple." And in verse 2: "They have left the dead bodies of your servants... the flesh of your own people."

Your. Your. Your. Your.

Asaph is not simply reporting tragedy. He is saying, with the directness of a child running to his father: Papa, look what they did to Your house. He is reminding God — and anchoring himself — to the relationship. These were not just Israel's things. They were God's things. The servants were God's servants. The desecrated temple was God's dwelling place.

This is not a formal petition. This is intimate. It is the prayer of someone who, in the worst moment of their life, runs to the one person they believe is both able to do something and deeply invested in what has happened. You only cry out to someone you believe is listening. The lament is already the faith.

We sometimes imagine that strong faith looks composed. The kind that smiles through the rubble and says "God is good" with a steady voice even when everything is gone. There is a version of faith that performs peace so consistently that actual grief has nowhere to go.

But this psalm does not perform. Asaph weeps in public, writes it down, sings it in the assembly. And God preserved it in Scripture — which means God is not waiting for you to pull yourself together before He will listen. The raw, red-eyed prayer is welcome. The trembling voice is welcome. The "I don't know what to say, but here I am" is welcome.

Maybe the gray-sky morning you are sitting inside right now is exactly the kind of morning God is leaning in to hear from you. Not the polished version of you. Not the one with the composed prayer and the composed face. The version sitting in the rubble, looking at what used to be, saying quietly: Father, look what happened.

That is enough. That is already faith.

Psalm 79 does not end at verse 4. By verse 9, Asaph has moved from description to petition: "Help us, God our Savior, for the glory of your name; deliver us and forgive our sins for your name's sake." (Psalm 79:9, NIV). He started in desolation. He ended reaching upward.

That is the shape of grief that survives — not the grief that never cries, but the grief that cries toward someone. The temple may be ruins. The street may be flooded. The neighbors may still be watching. But the direction of the cry matters. Asaph aimed his grief toward heaven, and heaven was already leaning in.

You do not need the right words. You do not need composure. You need only to begin the way Asaph began: O God. Two words. A whole prayer already lives there.

Today, if something sacred in your life has been broken — a relationship, a hope, a season you thought God had promised — borrow Asaph's prayer. Open Psalm 79. Read it out loud. Let his ancient grief carry your present one toward the same God who received it then.

The psalmist held on by praying. You can too.

articles.title: The Math God Does Differently
articles.dek: Solomon told his son to honor God with the firstfruits — before counting what was left. That one word quietly rewrites the order most of us live by.
articles.quote: Firstfruits giving is not a formula you run after the harvest. It is a prayer you plant before it.
articles.body:
Maria owns a small sari-sari store just off the main road in Batangas — the kind with a steel grille window and a handwritten price list taped to the inside. Every Monday morning, especially during habagat season when foot traffic slows and people stay home, she faces the same question before she rolls up the window. She opens her tin cashbox. She counts what is left from last week. She tallies what she owes for inventory. She sets aside the rent. And then — only after all that — she decides how much she can give to God.

It makes complete sense. It feels responsible. Kasi naman — you have to know if there is enough before you can give, right? That logic is bulletproof. And it is exactly the logic that Proverbs 3 quietly, gently dismantles.

Proverbs 3 is part of a longer instruction from a father to his son — the kind of conversation Filipino fathers give, or wish they had given, before their child steps out into the world on their own. Sit down. Listen. Here is what I have learned. The chapter opens with one of the most-quoted passages in all of Scripture: "Trust in the Lord with all your heart and lean not on your own understanding; in all your ways submit to him, and he will make your paths straight" (Proverbs 3:5–6, NIV). Most people stop there, satisfied. But Solomon keeps going. By verse 7 he says: "Do not be wise in your own eyes; fear the Lord and shun evil" (v. 7, NIV). And by verse 9 he has moved from spiritual posture into very concrete, practical territory: money, harvests, and the order in which you give.

That shift is intentional. Solomon was not writing two different sermons and stapling them together. He was drawing a straight line from the spiritual to the material: how you handle your money is a direct expression of how much you actually trust God. The person who says "I trust God" but reorganizes their finances so that God gets only the leftovers has not yet fully understood what trust means. Trust shows up in the order.

Honor the Lord with your wealth, with the firstfruits of all your crops; then your barns will be filled to overflowing, and your vats will brim over with new wine. — Proverbs 3:9–10, NIV

Here is what most people miss about firstfruits.

In ancient Israel, firstfruits were not a tithe calculated after the harvest was safely brought in. You did not count every grain, divide by ten, and hand over your percentage. The firstfruits were the very first sheaves cut from the standing field — offered before the threshing floor was laid out, before the total yield was known, before you had any idea whether this would be a good year or a lean one. You gave when you did not yet know if there was enough.

This is what makes firstfruits giving genuinely difficult. It is not primarily a financial discipline. It is a theological statement. When you bring the first sheaf to God before counting the rest, you are declaring out loud: God, you are the source of this harvest, not me. I will honor you with the first of it before I know how much I have, because I trust that you are the God of enough.

That is also why this command lives right beside "lean not on your own understanding" (v. 5, NIV). Giving the firstfruits and leaning not on your own understanding are not two separate principles. They are the same principle, applied to two different areas of life. The person who can hand over the first sheaf before counting the rest has genuinely stopped leaning on their own understanding about money. The person who cannot quite bring themselves to do it — who keeps waiting until the numbers make sense first — is still, quietly, leaning.

And there is a deeper resonance here that makes this verse tremble with meaning.

The New Testament calls Jesus himself "the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep" (1 Corinthians 15:20, NIV). When the Father raised His Son from the dead, He did not wait to see how many people would believe. He did not calculate the yield of the harvest first — how many nations, how many generations, how many Filipinos in Batangas in 2026. He raised Jesus before any of it was counted. Jesus was given and raised as the firstfruits, the pledge and foretaste of what was coming, offered before the full harvest was in. We give firstfruits because God already did. Every time you honor God with your first before you know if there is enough, you are enacting the same logic that held the universe together on Easter morning.

So what does this look like, practically, this Monday in July?

It starts with a reorder. Not a formula — a posture. Before you open the cashbox and count what is left, set aside your offering first. Before you check your bank app and calculate what you can spare, put God's portion at the top of the list. Not because this triggers a divine blessing algorithm. Not because God needs your money. But because the order you give in is a confession of who you trust.

And firstfruits thinking expands beyond money. Does God get the first hour of your morning, or only what remains after you have scrolled, rushed, and arrived at work already depleted? Do you bring God your freshest attention, or only the dregs of a tired mind at bedtime? The principle is the same across every area of life: give God the first. The best. The not-yet-counted.

If the thought of that makes your stomach tighten — if your immediate reaction is "but what if there isn't enough?" — sit with that feeling. That anxiety is a signal. It tells you exactly where you are still leaning on your own understanding rather than trusting Him. The verse does not promise you will never feel the pinch. It promises overflow. But the overflow comes after the faithfulness. The giving comes first.

Maria still opens her tin cashbox every Monday morning. But she changed the order. Before she counts, she sets aside the first. Some Mondays the math still feels tight. Some weeks she honestly does not know how the grocery bill will work out before Friday.

But she has found something the ledger does not show: a peace that settles in when you stop waiting until you know if there is enough. When you give first, you are not being reckless. You are practicing the same theology that split the darkness on Easter Sunday morning — something given before the full count was in, before the harvest was numbered, before anyone knew how large it would all become.

Firstfruits giving is not a formula you run after the harvest. It is a prayer you plant before it.

That is the math God does differently. And it starts, as it always does, with trust.
